coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of highly conductive carbon quantum dot-enhanced Z-scheme BiOBr/g-C3N4 heterojunction for effective photocatalytic degradation of tetracycline hydrochloride
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Physics and Chemistry of Solids
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Jin
Qin, Suqing
Yang, Xiaofeng
Wang, Yueying
Yang, Tai
Que, Meidan
Ma, Yuzhao
Li, Yanjun
coredata.dc:description: BiOBr can effectively mitigate the ecological and environmental problems caused by pollution with antibiotics, and its photocatalytic properties can be improved by utilizing heterojunction structures and cocatalysts. In this study, the Z-scheme heterojunction ternary photocatalyst BiOBr/g-C3N4/CQDs (BCCx) was obtained by using an uncomplicated electrostatic self-assembly process. The incorporation of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with high conductivity provided a conduit for charge transfer between g-C3N4 and BiOBr. The loading density of CQDs on BiOBr/g-C3N4 could be rapidly adjusted by changing the concentration of the CQDs precursor in solution. The presence of CQDs enhanced the absorption of visible light and inhibited the synthesis of photogenerated charge carriers, thereby resulting in a synergistic effect on the Z-scheme heterojunction and improving the photocatalytic properties under visible light. In particular, BCC50 had the highest catalytic performance, with a tetracycline pollutant degradation efficiency of 100% within 60 min. The degradation efficiency remained above 95% even after repeated use in five cycles. The principles of semiconductor energy bands and internal electrostatic field generation are applied to explain the photocatalytic mechanism responsible for tetracycline degradation using BiOBr/g-C3N4/CQDs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: BiOBr
g-C3N4
CQDs
Z-Scheme heterojunction
Photocatalytic degradation

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Physics and Chemistry of Solids

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was financially supported by the Scientific Research Program Funded by Shaanxi Provincial Education Department (No. 21JY021 to Xiaofeng Yang) and the Natural Science Basic Research Plan in Shaanxi Province (No. 2023-JC-YB-454 to Xiaofeng Yang).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Zeta potentials for BiOBr/g-C3N4 and BCC50 dispersed in DI water.
(a) XRD patterns obtained for g-C3N4, BiOBr, and BCCx (x = 0, 10, 20, 50, 80, 100). (b) XRD patterns obtained for CQDs. (c) FTIR spectra obtained for pristine BiOBr and BC, and BCC50.
XPS images of BiOBr and BCC50.
SEM images of (a) BiOBr, (b) g-C3N4, (c) BiOBr/g-C3N4, and (d) BCC50.
(a) EDS spectrum and (b–f) elemental mapping for the BCC50 composite.
(a) TEM and (b) high resolution TEM images of BCC50.
(a) UV–Vis DRS spectra obtained for BiOBr, BiOBr/g-C3N4, and BCC. (b) Tauc image of BiOBr. Valence spectra obtained for (c) BiOBr and (d) g-C3N4. Mott–Schottky plots for (e) BiOBr and (f) g-C3N4.
(a) Photocurrent and (b) EIS images for BiOBr, BiOBr/g-C3N4, and BCC50.
(a) TC-HCl degradation curve and (b) kinetic curves fitted for BC0–BCC100. Effect of (c) catalyst dosage and (d) initial solution pH on TC degradation of BCC50.
(a) TC-HCl cyclic degradation test results, (b) comparison of BCC50 before and after cycling, and (c) SEM image of BCC50 after use.

(a) Test results with different trapping agents during photocatalytic degradation process with BCC50. ESR spectra obtained for (b) DMPO-·O2 − and (c) DMPO-·OH using BCC50. (d) TC-HCl degradation mechanism using BCC50.

head.ce:title: Synthesis of highly conductive carbon quantum dot-enhanced Z-scheme BiOBr/g-C3N4 heterojunction for effective photocatalytic degradation of tetracycline hydrochloride

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Materials Science and Engineering, Xi'an University of Architecture and Technology, Xi'an, 710055, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Materials Science and Engineering
Xi'an University of Architecture and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Materials Science and Engineering, Xi’an University of Architecture and Technology, Xi’an 710055, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: BiOBr can effectively mitigate the ecological and environmental problems caused by pollution with antibiotics, and its photocatalytic properties can be improved by utilizing heterojunction structures and cocatalysts. In this study, the Z-scheme heterojunction ternary photocatalyst BiOBr/g-C3N4/CQDs (BCCx) was obtained by using an uncomplicated electrostatic self-assembly process. The incorporation of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with high conductivity provided a conduit for charge transfer between g-C3N4 and BiOBr. The loading density of CQDs on BiOBr/g-C3N4 could be rapidly adjusted by changing the concentration of the CQDs precursor in solution. The presence of CQDs enhanced the absorption of visible light and inhibited the synthesis of photogenerated charge carriers, thereby resulting in a synergistic effect on the Z-scheme heterojunction and improving the photocatalytic properties under visible light. In particular, BCC50 had the highest catalytic performance, with a tetracycline pollutant degradation efficiency of 100% within 60 min. The degradation efficiency remained above 95% even after repeated use in five cycles. The principles of semiconductor energy bands and internal electrostatic field generation are applied to explain the photocatalytic mechanism responsible for tetracycline degradation using BiOBr/g-C3N4/CQDs.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Achieving sustainable development has become the main goal for humanity [1–3]. However, the rapid development of pharmaceutical and animal husbandry industries has led to the discharge of large quantities of antibiotics and their intermediate metabolites into ecological water systems, thereby posing a significant threat to human health. Photocatalytic techniques have emerged as viable solutions to address the ecological and environmental problems caused by pesticide pollution [4–6]. In particular, the utilization of semiconductor photocatalysis for degrading pollutants is an economical, eco-friendly, and efficient approach for mitigating the current environmental crisis [7,8]. BiOBr is important in the field of photocatalysis because of its visible light responsiveness due to a relatively narrow band gap structure [9]. In addition, the interactions between weak interlayer van der Waals forces and robust in-plane covalent bond forces allow the facile crystallization of BiOBr into anisotropic two-dimensional (2D) nanosheets. This layered crystal structure is suitable for the rational structural design of Bi–O and Br− [10,11]. However, BiOBr photocatalytic materials are still affected by issues such as low quantum efficiency, facile electron–hole pair recombination, and weak reduction capacities [12,13]. Common strategies for enhancing the photocatalytic performance of BiOBr include elemental doping, defect engineering, and heterostructure construction [14,15].

Many investigations have demonstrated that heterojunction construction is a successful method for enhancing the charge transfer rate, promoting the separation of electron–hole pairs, and increasing the photocatalytic capacity of a single photocatalyst [16]. g-C3N4 is a non-metallic organic polymer semiconductor photocatalyst with high physical and chemical stability, excellent optical properties, and a suitable band gap width (∼2.7 eV), and it is also simple to synthesize. Consequently, g-C3N4 has widespread applications in the photocatalytic degradation of contaminants [17–19]. Numerous studies have demonstrated that the formation of a heterostructure between BiOBr and g-C3N4 can effectively promote carrier transfer and separation, thereby significantly enhancing the catalytic activity under visible light [20–22]. The construction of an appropriate energy band between BiOBr and g-C3N4 can place photogenerated carriers in their most stable energy configuration [23]. In addition, the bulk disordered nanostructure of g-C3N4 and ordered layered nanostructure of BiOBr favor the formation of a 2D-2D heterojunction [16]. However, carrier recombination still limits the photocatalytic activity of this complex.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are micro-spherical materials with a diameter of less than 10 nm, which possess unique light absorption characteristics that allow them to effectively broaden the absorption range of photocatalysts to the solar spectrum [24]. Studies have demonstrated that CQDs can exhibit excellent electrical conductivity, transfer photogenerated electrons to the surface of catalysts, and facilitate photocatalytic reactions [25]. In addition, the unique π–π conjugated structure of CQDs can play a role in separating electron–hole pairs due to oxygen vacancies [26]. CQDs can also facilitate the connection of functional groups with distinct characteristics during the preparation process, thereby leading to various surface defects that allow the photocatalyst to efficiently capture and release photogenerated electrons [27]. For example, Zhang et al. employed CQDs as a modifier to increase the absorption of visible light and separation of carriers in a BiOBr/Bi2WO6 composite catalysts [28].

In this study, we employed CQDs as the charge transfer medium and fabricated a novel Z-scheme BiOBr/g-C3N4/CQDs (BCCx) heterojunction photocatalyst via the electrostatic self-assembly technique. The photocatalytic properties of BCCx were characterized based on the degradation of tetracycline hydrochloride (TC-HCl). The results showed that the addition of CQDs to the BCCx photocatalyst improved its photocatalytic activity under irradiation by a xenon lamp. Furthermore, our investigation of the effect of CQDs loading on the performance of BCCx demonstrated the potentially effective use of cocatalysts in photocatalysis.

Analytically pure grade (99%) chemicals were utilized in this study, including bismuth nitrate hydrate (Bi(NO3)3·5H2O), cetyl trimethyl ammonium bromide, potassium bromide (KBr), citric acid monohydrate (C6H8O7·H2O), and melamine (C3H6N6). These chemicals were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Corporation and Aladdin Industrial Corporation, and used without further purification. Deionized (DI) water was used in the experiments.

g-C3N4 was synthesized by calcining dicyandiamide in a muffle furnace at 550 °C for 5 h [29]. Typically, 30 mg of g-C3N4 and 0.601 g of KBr (5 mmol) were first dispersed in a certain amount of DI water for 30 min with magnetic stirring to obtain a homogeneous solution (solution A). Next, 2.4255 g of Bi(NO3)3·5H2O (5 mmol) was stirred into 100 mL of mixed solution (acetic acid:DI water = 1:9), and then stirred for 15 min (solution B). The mixed solution B was slowly poured into solution A and agitated for 1 h at room temperature. The suspension was allowed to stand and age for 4 h prior to filtration. The resultant precipitate was then collected, repeatedly cleaned with DI water and absolute ethyl alcohol, and vacuum dried at 60 °C for 12 h.

First, 3.0 g of C6H8O7•H2O was dissolved in 45 mL of DI water and agitated for 30 min at room temperature. NH3·H2O was then added to the solution in a dropwise manner while regulating the pH of the solution to a neutral level. This suspension was placed into an autoclave with a Teflon lining and maintained at 180 °C for 8 h. When the reaction finished, the autoclave was cooled down to 25 °C. Subsequently, the reaction solution was transferred to a 3500 Da dialysis bag and soaked in a beaker containing DI water, where the dialysis bag was completely immersed in water. A homogeneous solution of CQDs was obtained by continuously stirring the dialysis bag while periodically refreshing the outer DI water, and CQDs powder was then obtained by freeze drying.

A series of BiOBr/g-C3N4/CQDs photocatalysts were prepared with various amounts of added CQDs by using an electrostatic self-assembly method. These photocatalysts were denoted as BCCx (x = 0, 10, 20, 50, 80, 100), where “B” represents flaky BiOBr, the first “C” represents g-C3N4, the second “C” represents CQDs, and “x” denotes the volume of CQDs solution included in BiOBr/g-C3N4.

Next, 0.5 g of BiOBr/g-C3N4 was dispersed in 30 mL of methanol solution and agitated for 30 min. To obtain consistent suspensions, the solutions were ultrasonicated for 15 min. Subsequently, 0 μL, 10 μL, 20 μL, 50 μL, 80 μL, or 100 μL of CQDs solution (30 mg L−1) was added to the white BiOBr/g-C3N4 suspension, and the mixture was stirred continuously in a water bath at 60 °C. Finally, the powder was collected to obtain the BiOBr/g-C3N4/CQDs composite catalyst after evaporating off the methanol.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and elemental mapping were conducted using an FEI Tecnai G2 F20 system, and scanning electron microscopy (SEM) with an FEI Quanta 250 FEG. X-ray diffraction (XRD) was conducted using a Bruker D8 Advance system to investigate the crystal structures of the materials. Fourier transform-infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy was performed with a Bruker (Germany) Vertex 70 system. A Thermo Fisher Scientific (Germany) Escalab 250Xi system was used to conduct X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). An ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) spectrophotometer (PerkinElmer Lambda 750, USA) was used for UV–Vis diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (UV–Vis DRS).

Photoelectrochemical measurements were acquired utilizing a three-electrode system with a CHI660E electrochemical analyzer (Shanghai Chen Hua Instrument Co., China). Typically, the electrolyte was 0.5 M Na2SO4 solution, the counter electrode was a Pt sheet, and the reference electrode was Ag/AgCl. The working electrode comprised glass with fluorine-doped tin oxide and Nafion. AC perturbation at 5 mV was applied during electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) testing at open circuit voltage.

TC-HCl degradation was used to test the photocatalytic activity of BCCx under exposure to a 500 W xenon lamp. In order to create an adsorption–desorption equilibrium, 40 mg of catalyst was typically disseminated in TC-HCl solution (10 mg L−1, 40 mL), and the suspension was magnetically agitated in the dark for 30 min. During irradiation, samples measuring approximately 3 mL were collected for filtration. After filtration, the solution was placed in a cuvette and the level of degradation was determined by UV–Vis spectrophotometry. The catalytic degradation effect was evaluated by calculating the degradation efficiency and degradation rate constant.

Controlled studies were conducted by utilizing different scavengers under conditions similar to those in the photocatalytic degradation process in order to identify the active species involved in the photocatalytic processes. In the experiments, 1 mmol L−1 of tert-butanol (TBA), ammonium oxalate (AO), and p-benzoquinone (P-BQ) were added to specifically capture hydroxyl radicals (•OH), photogenerated holes (h+), and superoxide radicals (•O2 −), respectively.

The process used to synthesize BiOBr/g-C3N4/CQDs is illustrated in Scheme 1 . The electrostatic layer-by-layer self-assembly technique utilizes the electrostatic interactions between two polyelectrolytes with opposite charges to facilitate adsorption onto a surface-treated charged solid matrix, thereby resulting in the formation of a nanoscale polymer [30]. The BiOBr/g-C3N4/CQDs composite photocatalyst was synthesized using the electrostatic self-assembly process with negatively charged CQDs [31] and BiOBr/g-C3N4 nanosheets, with methanol as the solution, as shown in Fig. 1 . The zeta potential decreased from +9.44 mV (BiOBr/g-C3N4) to +1.23 mV (BCC50) because the amount of CQDs added was small and they did not cover the entire surface of BiOBr/g-C3N4. Thus, some H+ ions were still present on the surface of BiOBr/g-C3N4 and the nanocomposite generally exhibited positive polarity. The results demonstrated that CQDs were successfully absorbed on the surface of BiOBr/g-C3N4 through the electrostatic self-assembly strategy.

XRD patterns were obtained for the synthesized catalysts in order to identify their structures (Fig. 2 a). The XRD patterns obtained for the photocatalysts with various amounts of added CQDs produced characteristic diffraction peaks at 10.9°, 21.9°, 25.2°, 31.7°, 32.2°, 39.4°, 46.2°, and 57.1°, which were assigned to the (001), (002), (101), (102), (110), (112), (200), and (212) crystal planes of the pure BiOBr (PDF#09–0393), respectively [32,33]. According to JCPDS 87–1526, the two diffraction peaks observed at 13.1° and 27.4° in pure g-C3N4 could be attributed to the (100) and (002) phases, respectively, thereby indicating the in-plane structure of the accumulated aliphatic chain segments and interfacial regions [34–36]. A broad peak at around 25° was clearly visible in the XRD pattern obtained for the CQDs (Fig. 2b), which was consistent with the (002) lattice plane of carbon-based materials [37]. However, due to the small amounts of g-C3N4 and CQDs in the composite catalyst, the corresponding diffraction peaks could not be observed in the XRD pattern.

The FTIR spectra obtained for BiOBr, BC, and BCC50 are shown in Fig. 2c. The absorption band at 514 cm−1 was due to the Bi–O stretching mode in BiOBr [38–40]. The absorption peaks observed at 809 cm−1 in the FTIR spectra for the BC and BCC50 complexes corresponded to the bending vibration of the s-triazine unit in g-C3N4 [41], and multiple peaks located within the range of 1200–1700 cm−1 were attributed to the typical expansion modes of C–N heterocycles in g-C3N4, and thus the molecular composition of g-C3N4 remained unchanged [42,43]. The absorption peaks observed at 2850 cm−1 and 2918 cm−1 in BCC50 were due to the tensile vibrations of N–H and C–H [44], and the wide peaks between 3000 and 3500 cm−1 were related to the tensile vibration of –OH caused by the introduction of CQDs in the composite catalyst [45]. In addition, the Bi–O telescopic vibration peaks for BiOBr in the composites exhibited some deviations, thereby indicating mutual effects between BiOBr, CQDs, and g-C3N4.

XPS was conducted to determine the chemical bond compositions and surface interactions of diverse complexes in the pure BiOBr and BCC50 samples. Fig. 3 shows the comprehensive XPS spectra for the BiOBr and BCC50 photocatalysts, as well as the detailed spectra for C, N, Br, O, and Bi elements. The surface of the pure BiOBr was analyzed to detect the presence of C, Br, O, and Bi elements. The detection of elemental C was attributed to instrumental contamination. C, N, Br, O, and Bi elements were detected in the BCC50 composite, where O, Br, and Bi were derived from BiOBr, C originated from g-C3N4 and CQDs, and N was detected in its elemental form. The chemical status and binding energies of C 1s, N 1s, Bi 4f, Br 3d, and O 1s were determined using the high-resolution XPS spectra. The C 1s XPS spectrum (Fig. 3b) for BCC50 had three signals at 284.8 eV, 286.4 eV, and 288.7 eV, which were ascribed to the C–C bond due to carbon contamination of the instrument, the C–OH bond on the surface of the CQDs, and the N–CN bond in g-C3N4, respectively [46,47]. As shown in Fig. 3c, the peaks for N–CN, N-(C)3, and C–N–H in CN appeared at 398.9 eV, 399.7 eV, and 400.6 eV, respectively [48,49]. The O 1s XPS spectrum (Fig. 3d) contained peaks for Bi–O and O–O at 530.1 eV and 531.6 eV in BiOBr, respectively, and at 530.0 eV and 531.9 eV in BCC50, and they were due to the change in the internal charge density of the catalyst caused by the combination of BiOBr and CQDs. The binding energy peak at 533.4 eV was related to the –OH formed by the addition of CQDs [50,51]. Fig. 3e shows that the peaks for Br 3d5/2 and Br 3d3/2 for Br− in BCC50 were at energies of 68.3 eV and 69.3 eV, respectively. Fig. 3f shows that the peaks for Bi 4f5/2 and Bi 4f7/2 of Bi3+ were at 164.5 and 159.2 eV, respectively, according to the Bi 4f spectra for BCC50 [52,53]. Compared with the binding energy peak for BiOBr, the offset of the peak position for the composite catalyst indicated that the contact surface of the composite material formed a chemical bond and the chemical environment changed.

Analysis of the proportions of elements in the synthesized materials indicated significant abundances of Bi, O, and Br compared with C and N (Fig. 5a). However, EDS elemental mapping (Fig. 5b–f) demonstrated that BCC50 had suitable distributions of C, N, O, Br, and Bi elements, thereby indicating that its manufacture was effective. The addition of minor amounts of g-C3N4 and CQDs resulted in considerable reductions in the brightness of C and N elements.

The TEM image of the BCC50 photocatalyst in Fig. 6 a shows that there was a uniform distribution of numerous small gray spots on the sheet structure of BiOBr/g-C3N4, which indicated the presence of CQDs. The lattice distance values of 0.201 nm, 0.323 nm, and 0.326 nm corresponded to the (004) plane of BiOBr, (002) plane of g-C3N4, and (002) crystal plane of the CQDs, respectively, thereby confirming the successful preparation of BCC50, as shown in Fig. 6b.

The photo-absorption properties of BiOBr, BiOBr/g-C3N4, and BCCx were evaluated by UV–Vis DRS (Fig. 7 a). In contrast to the pure BiOBr, the absorption spectrum for the BiOBr/g-C3N4 composite exhibited a slight red shift and enhanced absorption intensity in the visible region. After adding CQDs, the absorption peak on the left side of the absorption curve for the BCCx photocatalyst changed little. However, the addition of CQDs increased the absorption intensity in the visible region on the right due to their excellent capacity for absorbing visible light. The photocatalyst exhibited the optimal light response performance with a total CQDs amount of 50 μL. However, when the loading of CQDs exceeded 50 μL, the light absorption capacity weakened, possibly because the excessive amount of CQDs covered the original light response sites on the surface of the BiOBr/g-C3N4 composite catalyst. In addition, the excessive surface density of CQDs may have formed a recombination center for photogenerated carriers to decrease the quantum utilization efficiency.

The plots of (αhν)1/2 versus hν in Fig. 7b show that the bandgap energy (Eg) value for the pure BiOBr was 2.54 eV. Fig. 7c and d shows the valence spectra for BiOBr and g-C3N4, respectively. The E VB
XPS values for BiOBr and g-C3N4 were 2.26 eV and 2.08 eV, respectively. The valence band (VB) values for BiOBr and g-C3N4 were 2.37 eV and 2.19 eV, respectively, according to formula (1) [54]. In addition, the flat-band potential (F b ) values for BiOBr and g-C3N4 were tested at 1000 Hz. As shown in Fig. 7e and f, the F b values for BiOBr and g-C3N4 were −0.37 eV and −0.60 eV, respectively. According to formula (2) [55,56], the conduction band (CB) values for BiOBr and g-C3N4 were −0.17 eV and −0.40 eV, respectively. According to formula (3), the Eg value for BiOBr was 2.54 eV, as shown in Fig. 7b.
(1) E VB
NHE = φ + E VB
XPS – 4.44
(2) E CB
NHE = E Ag/AgCl + 0.197
(3) E g = E VB
NHE – E CB
NHE

The photocurrent curves and EIS spectra obtained for BiOBr, BiOBr/g-C3N4, and BCC50 are presented in Fig. 8 . Fig. 8a shows that the best light response was clearly observed for BCC50. After exposure to light, the current density in the photocatalyst reached a maximum of 0.0004 μA cm−2. In particular, the photocurrent increased in the order of BiOBr, BiOBr/g-C3N4, and then BCC50, thereby indicating that adding the CQDs and g-C3N4 enhanced the photocatalytic performance. Moreover, the EIS plots (Fig. 8b) indicated lower charge transfer resistance in BCC50 because the diameter of the semicircle obtained for BCC50 was significantly smaller than those for BiOBr and BiOBr/g-C3N4 [57,58]. These results indicate that the incorporation of CQDs facilitated the transfer of photogenerated carriers to the surface of the catalyst to participate in redox reactions, which corresponded with the catalytic degradation effect.

The photocatalytic capacities and chemical stabilities of the BiOBr/g-C3N4/CQDs heterojunction photocatalysts with various CQDs contents were evaluated by assessing the photographic degradation of TC-HCl under irradiation by visible light. The dark adsorption experiment was conducted for 30 min to achieve an equilibrium adsorption state before irradiation with visible light. In the degradation experiment (Fig. 9 a), the BCCx photocatalysts degraded all of the TC-HCl pollutant within 120 min. The addition of CQDs improved the efficiency of TC-HCl removal by the BCCx heterojunction photocatalysts compared with the BC catalyst. In particular, the BCC50 photocatalyst obtained the best degradation performance, with a degradation rate of 99.63% within 50 min and the complete degradation of TC-HCl within 60 min. The addition of CQDs accelerated the photodegradation of the pollutant by improving the separation and transfer of photogenerated carriers in BCCx. However, adding a higher concentration of CQDs (>50 μL) decreased the rate of photodegradation by the BCCx photocatalysts. Therefore, optimizing the ratio between the CQDs and BC could enhance the efficiency of photodegradation. However, BCC50 successfully degraded the pollutant within 60 min, so kinetic fitting was not conducted at this stage. The kinetic fitting diagrams for the photocatalysts are shown in Fig. 9b. The kinetic curves for –ln(C0/Ct) and time (t) of TC-HCl degradation show that the degradation constants for the BC composite catalysts increased to different degrees after adding CQDs, and thus the activities of the photocatalysts improved. The maximum kinetic constant was 0.09217 min−1 for BCC50, which was 2.07 times than that for the BC without CQDs. Therefore, the pollutant degradation performance was best for the BCC50 composite catalyst.

In order to better evaluate the applicability of the catalyst and to optimize its photocatalytic performance, we tested the photocatalytic degradation performance of the catalyst in different solutions [59–61]. Fig. 9c shows the effects of different catalyst dosages on the degradation of TC-HCl. As the catalyst concentration increased from 0.5 g L−1 to 1.0 g L−1, the degradation rate of TC-HCl by BCC50 increased from 94.16% to 100%, and the degradation kinetic constant increased from 0.06003 min−1 to 0.09217 min−1. However, when the amount of catalyst added increased to 1.5 g L−1 and 2.0 g L−1, the degradation rate decreased slightly with BCC50, which indicates that adding an appropriate amount of catalyst was conducive to promoting the pollutant photodegradation reaction. Adding the catalyst at insufficient or excessive amounts could adversely impact the active sites and electron transfer in the photocatalytic reaction to reduce the overall photocatalytic performance [60]. The pH of the suspension was adjusted by using 0.1 M NaOH or HCl, and the degradation efficiencies of the catalyst at different pH values are shown in Fig. 9d. In the pH range of 1–13, the degradation rate of TC-HCl with BCC50 did not change significantly, and thus the photocatalytic activity of BCC50 was stable over almost the entire pH range.

The degradation of the pollutant using the BCC50 composite catalyst after five repeated test cycles is illustrated in Fig. 10 a. The photocatalytic performance declined slightly after five cycles, possibly due to the occupation of active sites by pollutants and the subsequent inhibition of reactions. In addition, the XRD and SEM profiles obtained before and after using the catalyst are shown in Fig. 10b and c. The XRD spectrum for the catalyst indicated that no new diffraction peaks appeared and there were no significant changes in the peak positions. The BCC50 catalyst still retained its sheet-like aggregated structure even after five cycles with no significant changes compared with its unused state, thereby indicating its high cycle stability and reusability.

TBA (·OH, 1 mM) [55,62], P-BQ (·O2 −, 1 mM), and AO (h+, 1 mM) were employed as active radical scavengers to determine the active species responsible for TC-HCl degradation by BCC50, as shown in Fig. 11 a. The addition of AO decreased the TC-HCl degradation rate from 100% to 83.75%. Similarly, the rates decreased to 92.74% and 7.26% after the addition of TBA and P-BQ, respectively. These findings suggest that ·O2 − played a predominant role in the photocatalytic reaction. Furthermore, electron paramagnetic resonance (ESR) demonstrated that ·O2 − generation was stopped by the spin-trapping quencher 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO) in the photocatalytic process. As shown in Fig. 11b and c, the characteristic ESR peak was not found under dark conditions. However, the ·O2 − signal was observed after irradiation by visible light, which indicated the generation of ·O2 − radicals during the photocatalytic process. In addition, the ·OH peak did not change significantly, and thus ·O2 − had the main role in the photocatalytic reaction.

The mechanism responsible for tetracycline pollutant degradation by the photocatalyst BCC50 is shown in Fig. 11d. Based on the XPS VB spectrum, Mott–Schottky curve, and UV–Vis DRS test results, the CB and VB values were estimated for the BiOBr sheet as −0.17 eV and 2.37 eV, respectively, and those for g-C3N4 were −0.40 eV and 2.19 eV. For the pure g-C3N4, photoinduced electrons could have reduced O2 to form ·O2 − due to the more negative level of E CB (−0.40 eV) than the redox potential of O2/·O2 − (−0.33 eV). In addition, the VB potentials for BiOBr and g-C3N4 were less positive than E0(H2O/·OH) (+2.72 eV), so the h+ in the VB of BiOBr and g-C3N4 was incapable of oxidizing H2O to ·OH. Free radical capture experiments demonstrated that ·O2 − was the primary active species in the catalytic reaction. Thus, the Z-type heterojunction structure formed between g-C3N4 and BiOBr facilitated electron transfer from the CB of BiOBr to the VB of g-C3N4, and promoted their re-excitation to further participate in the reaction. The CQDs acted as a charge transmission bridge to facilitate efficient electron transfer between the two catalysts, BiOBr and g-C3N4. However, due to the challenging positioning of CQDs between the two catalysts, some electrons that transitioned to the CB could have directly participated in the catalytic reactions with CQDs.

In this study, the BiOBr/g-C3N4/CQDs (BCCx) photocatalyst was successfully prepared by using the electrostatic self-assembly method. Compared with the pure BiOBr/g-C3N4, the performance of the BiOBr/g-C3N4/CQDs heterojunction was greater in the photocatalytic degradation of TC-HCl. In particular, the BCC50 heterojunction had the highest photocatalytic activity with a degradation rate of 99.63% within 50 min, and the complete degradation of TC-HCl was achieved in 60 min, which was attributed to the inhibition of photogenerated carriers and enhanced light absorption. The results showed that ·O2 − was the main active species in the photocatalytic degradation reaction system. During this process, CQDs acted as electron transmission channels connecting BiOBr and g-C3N4, thereby facilitating the efficient directional transfer of electrons from the CB of BiOBr to CN, which improved the photocatalytic efficiency by preventing the recombination of photogenerated electron–hole pairs. These findings have significant implications for addressing complex environmental challenges.

Jin Chen: Writing – review & editing, Project administration, Conceptualization. Suqing Qin: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation. Xiaofeng Yang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Conceptualization. Yueying Wang: Investigation, Data curation. Tai Yang: Data curation. Meidan Que: Project administration, Conceptualization. Yuzhao Ma: Project administration. Yanjun Li: Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was financially supported by the Scientific Research Program Funded by Shaanxi Provincial Education Department (No. 21JY021 to Xiaofeng Yang) and the Natural Science Basic Research Plan in Shaanxi Province (No. 2023-JC-YB-454 to Xiaofeng Yang).
